text: The patient is positioned in steep Trendelenburg, with the cecum visible on the left side of the screen, indicating the orientation for the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon tucks the bowel into the left upper quadrant and begins unraveling the mesentery to expose the root of the mesentery. Using cautery, the surgeon carefully incises the window posterior to the ileocolic pedicle, revealing the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum as a landmark in the slightly obese patient, facilitating the visualization of the ileocolic pedicle for high ligation during the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure, revealing its fine attachments to the duodenum. The duodenum is reflected downward, exposing the head of the pancreas and the kidney with surrounding perinephric fat visible beyond the duodenum.

The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic pedicle, carefully exposing the ileocolic vein while avoiding the superior mesenteric vein. The vein is visible, with the artery located behind it, and the shadowing provides an impression of the superior mesenteric vein's position.
The surgeon uses a stapler to perform a high ligation of the iliocolic pedicle, dividing it and allowing for upward reflection of the tissue.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral edge of the duodenum, ensuring to stay anterior to the kidney. A thin film of tissue separates the lateral abdominal wall from the operative field. The duodenum is fully mobilized, with the pancreatic head visible. The ureter is identified, and the bowel is flipped up to the upper abdomen to facilitate later transverse colon mobilization.

The surgeon performs inferior mobilization of the mesentery, carefully avoiding entry into the retroperitoneum and keeping the ureter visible. The dissection is extended up to the duodenum, which becomes visible in the surgical field. Lateral mobilization follows, with attention to maintaining the correct plane and avoiding mobilization of the kidney.

The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking from the patient's colonoscopy on the colon. Using careful retraction, the colon is positioned medially as a midline structure on the right side. The surgeon prepares to dissect through the mesentery using an energy device, aiming to preserve the terminal ileum before firing a stapler across the small bowel.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to supine with right side rotation and separates the colon from the omentum. Adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy are taken down to expose the anterior surface of the duodenum. Clips on the cystic duct are visible. The thin tissue layer requires minimal mobilization to complete the hepatic flexure, using an energy device.

The surgeon carefully dissects fine attachments of the mesentery from the duodenum in an obese patient, aiming to mobilize the plane between the transverse colon mesentery and the stomach. The posterior surface of the stomach is visible. The surgeon uses an energy device to mobilize the transverse colon mesentery, with the right branch of the middle colic artery coming into view and being taken with the energy device.
The surgeon prepares to divide the large bowel, with the resected and mobilized specimen being tucked up on top of the liver. The laparoscopic view confirms the absence of metastatic disease.

The surgeon carefully aligns the bowel, ensuring the transverse colon and small bowel mesentery are correctly oriented to avoid twisting. A stay suture is placed to line up the anastomotic line, followed by the performance of a colotomy.

The surgeon performs an enterotomy and introduces the stapler to create an anastomosis. A grasper is used to assist the stapler's alignment, ensuring proper approximation of the bowel. The stapler is articulated to achieve a precise closure, resulting in a well-formed common channel.

The surgeon closes the common channel using a two-oh braided absorbable suture, starting at the crotch of the anastomosis and stitching across the staple line to ensure definitive closure. Each suture bite is cinched with the needle to maintain tension on the anastomosis.
The surgeon places the needle through the tissue, precisely loading it at the intended location for suturing.

The surgeon completes the two-layer closure of the anastomosis, ensuring there is no twist in the loops of bowel and good approximation of both ends. The second layer is imbricated for added security, and the anastomosis appears tension-free.
